channel.title: Privacy on Dwight Spencer - 0x5DCBF78E3F9C3FE3
channel.description: Recent content in Privacy on Dwight Spencer - 0x5DCBF78E3F9C3FE3
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 Dwight Spencer. All rights reserved. AI training prohibited.

item.title: Age Verification Is Identity Capture
item.description: Age verification is the name on the label. The contents are different. What these systems capture is not a yes-or-no answer about whether a user meets an age threshold. They capture a name, a document number, the geometry of a face, and a timestamp linking all three to a specific platform session. That is identity verification. The age assertion is incidental. The distinction matters technically. Age is a boolean: over threshold or not.

item.title: Owning Your Memory: Hardened Arenas, Channels, and Structural OOP in Pure C99
item.description: Dependencies <stdio.h> — libc <stdint.h> — libc <string.h> — libc The complete dependency graph. Provenance is the C standard and the POSIX libc the OS ships. Coding standards in force The codebase applies a layered set of standards, each enforceable via OPA/Rego AST gates, LSP diagnostics, and CI policy checks before implementation code is written or merged. ISO C99 (ISO/IEC 9899:1999) — the base language standard. No GNU extensions, no __attribute__, no C11 atomics.

item.title: When the Government Buys the Model: AI, Section 702, and the Surveillance Stack
item.description: The first: EPIC reported in April that Anthropic — the company that makes Claude — was in conflict with the Department of Defense over AI tool usage, and that a New Yorker piece indicated Anthropic's tools were being used to analyze Section 702 data that includes Americans' communications. Section 702 of FISA is the authority that allows intelligence agencies to collect communications of foreign targets — with the acknowledged side effect of sweeping in American communications when those Americans are in contact with foreign targets.

item.title: Chatrie v. United States: The Cloud Storage Question the Supreme Court Has to Answer
item.description: On April 27, the Supreme Court heard arguments in Chatrie v. United States (No. 25-112). The case involves Okello Chatrie, convicted of a 2019 bank robbery in Virginia based in part on location data obtained from Google via a geofence warrant — a warrant directing Google to provide location data for all devices near a crime scene during a specific time window. Chatrie had Location History enabled. The warrant reached his data along with data from everyone else who happened to be near that credit union during that two-hour window.

item.title: Troy, NY: How a Surveillance Contract Captured a City
item.description: On March 19, 2026, roughly 150 Troy residents showed up to a city council meeting and the council tabled the Flock camera contract for review. Reasonable. Standard legislative process. On March 31, the contract auto-renewed. The council hadn't voted to cancel it. They'd voted to pause and review. Under the contract terms, that was enough. Flock's renewal clause required affirmative council action to cancel — not to renew. Inaction was consent.

item.title: neural.sh: A Shell Script Where a Plugin Used to Be
item.description: After installing ALE, I went looking at what else w0rp had written. neural.vim was there — a Vim plugin for ChatGPT integration, from the same author who built one of the most solid LSP implementations in the Vim ecosystem. It's well-built. It also assumes the editor is the unit of composition. If your workflow lives in Vim, that holds. If you're working across a shell, a mail client, a plumber script, and pipelines stitched together with POSIX glue, a plugin that can't be invoked outside the editor is a constraint, not a tool.

item.title: After the Canary: What Happened to the Warrant Canary Standard
item.description: Canarytail is gone. In post 01, written in January 2025, I referenced Canarytail as an attempt to formalize the warrant canary into a machine-readable standard. As of May 2026, the canarytail GitHub organization does not resolve. The website canarytail.org returns nothing. The org appears to have been deleted entirely — not archived, not transferred, deleted. This post is the follow-up that situation demands. What Canarytail was The Canarytail standard proposed a structured JSON schema for warrant canary attestations — a machine-readable replacement for the freeform ASCII text files most sites publish.

item.title: Fourth Amendment in the Age of AI Surveillance
item.description: Written in my capacity as Technology Chair, Restore The Fourth. This is a personal technical brief, not legal advice, and does not constitute a formal position of Restore The Fourth national organization without separate ratification. Expert reviewed. Abstract The Fourth Amendment doctrine developed for physical searches is structurally inadequate for AI-powered mass surveillance. Automated License Plate Readers (ALPRs), predictive analytics platforms, and commercial data fusion are creating searchable records of population movement, association, and behavior at a scale and resolution that no warrant system was designed to govern.

item.title: Infrastructure Independence: Self-Hosting Without the Hype
item.description: Companion piece to Hacker Public Radio. Part of the Infrastructure Independence series. Abstract Self-hosting is having a moment. It's also attracting a lot of bad advice. This article cuts through the homelab fantasy to answer the questions that actually matter: what is worth hosting yourself, what is not, what the real threat model is, and how to build infrastructure that survives you being unavailable for two weeks. 1. The Honest Threat Model Most self-hosting guides start with "

item.title: The Watchers You Fed: Chapter Preview
item.description: The following is an excerpt from The Watchers You Fed (KDP, forthcoming). All rights reserved. No AI training. No reproduction without written permission. © 2026 Dwight Spencer · ORCID 0009-0001-0066-4646 Prologue: The Infrastructure You Built You didn't build the surveillance state. But you fed it. Every check-in, every search, every purchase routed through a platform's payment processor, every commute tracked by a license plate reader you drove past without noticing — these are not neutral transactions.

item.title: GitHub's TOS Won't Save You: Why Your Code, Privacy & Copyrights Are at Risk
item.description: The Illusion of Ownership Millions of developers trust GitHub with their most valuable creative work. But buried in its Terms of Service are provisions granting Microsoft remarkably broad rights over publicly hosted code — including the right to train commercial AI systems on it without attribution, compensation, or meaningful consent. Your open-source license is not a shield. GitHub's TOS acknowledges that you retain copyright over uploaded code. In practice, that statement coexists with a much wider set of rights GitHub grants itself:

item.title: Privacy Canaries: A Better Tweedy Bird
item.description: Warrent Canaries Update, May 2026: The Canarytail organization and website are no longer online. Post 09 documents what happened and what resilient canary infrastructure looks like without third-party dependencies. A warrant canary is a statement that declares that an organization has not taken certain actions or received certain requests for information from government or law enforcement authorities. Many services use warrant canaries to let users know how private their data is.
